title: Greetings & Openers
description: How Delhi people actually say hello — on the phone, in person, and in text.
culturalNote: Forget 'Namaste' for everyday use. Real Delhiites answer calls with 'Haan bata' and greet friends with 'Bol na' or 'Kya chal raha hai?' — not the formal greetings you'd find in a textbook. Namaste is reserved for elders, formal settings, and temples.

exercises.note: The default phone greeting in Delhi among friends. 'Bata' = tell me. When a friend calls and you pick up, 'Haan bata' = go ahead, I'm listening. Warm and casual.

exercises.english: Tell me / Speak up / Go on
exercises.note: 'Bol' = speak/tell (imperative). 'Na' softens it. 'Bol na yaar' = come on, tell me. Used when someone is hesitating, or as a casual 'go ahead' on the phone. Very common.

exercises.prompt: Your friend calls and you pick up. What's the most natural Delhi thing to say?
exercises.hint: The casual, real way friends answer each other's calls

exercises.english: How are you? / How's it going?
exercises.note: More personal than 'kaise ho'. 'Haal' = state/condition. You'd ask 'kya haal hai?' when you haven't seen someone in a while and genuinely want to know.
Quick check-in. Can be concerned ('sab theek?' when someone looks off) or casual (as a general greeting). Short, warm, versatile.
'Mast' = awesome / great / high. 'Haan mast' = yeah everything's awesome. Also just 'Mast!' alone works as 'great!' 'Sab mast hai' = everything's great.
'Bas theek hoon' signals you're okay but not great — not lying, not complaining. It's the honest middle ground. Friends will often follow up with 'kya hua?' (what happened?)

exercises.prompt: Friend asks 'Kya haal hai?' and you're doing really well. What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Chal raha hai' = is happening/going on (masculine). The feminine equivalent would be 'chal rahi hai'. Used as a greeting or genuinely asking what someone is up to.

exercises.english: After such a long time / Long time no see
exercises.note: Say this when you run into someone you haven't seen in a while. 'Din' = day, 'bahut dino baad' = after many days. Often followed by 'Kya haal hai?' or 'Kahan tha/thi tu?'

exercises.sentence: Arre yaar, _____ mili! Kya haal hai?
exercises.sentenceEnglish: Oh man, _____ we've met! How are you?

exercises.note: 'Kahan tha?' for a man. 'Kahan thi?' for a woman — the -a/-i ending changes with gender. 'Tu kahan thi?' = where were you? (informal, close friend)

exercises.prompt: You're asking your female friend where she was (you haven't seen her). Which form do you use?

exercises.note: Use with elders, strangers, formal contexts, or anyone you want to be respectful with. NOT what you'd say to a friend — that would feel weirdly formal. Often paired with hands folded (anjali mudra).

exercises.instruction: Match the greeting to when you'd use it

exercises.note: THE classic Delhi sign-off. 'Chal, bye!' ends nearly every phone call and in-person goodbye. You can add 'yaar': 'Chal yaar, bye!' = Alright man, bye! Simple, warm, universal.

exercises.english: We'll meet again / See you later
exercises.note: 'Milenge' = we will meet. Used as a warm goodbye when you don't know exactly when you'll see someone. More heartfelt than just 'chal bye'.

exercises.prompt: You're ending a call with a close friend in the most natural Delhi way. What do you say?
